metadata.title: Political Statements Timeline: Iran-Israel Conflict
metadata.description: A chronological collection of key political statements from world leaders regarding the Iran-Israel conflict, spanning from 2023 through early March 2026.
sources: U.S. Department of Defense Press Releases
Office of the Supreme Leader of Iran (khamenei.ir)
metadata.methodology: Statements were collected from official government communications, press conferences, verified social media accounts, and credible international news sources. Each statement has been reviewed for accuracy with a fact-check rating. All quotes are sourced from verified public records with clickable source links for independent verification.
metadata.disclaimer: All statements are sourced from official government communications, press conferences, and verified news reports. Quotes may be lightly paraphrased for clarity while preserving the speaker's documented position. Click source links to verify each statement independently. Fact-check ratings reflect editorial assessment based on available evidence.

statements.text: The Iran deal was the worst agreement ever negotiated. We got out and the world is safer because of it.
statements.context: Speaking at a campaign rally in Iowa, Trump reiterated his justification for withdrawing the U.S. from the JCPOA in 2018, claiming the decision improved global security.
statements.factCheckDetail: While Trump did withdraw from the JCPOA, multiple intelligence assessments indicated Iran resumed enrichment activities after the withdrawal, arguably reducing safety. The claim that the world is 'safer' is contested by most nonproliferation experts.

statements.text: Nobody wants peace more than me. I would have a deal with Iran done in one week if I were president.
statements.context: During a town hall event, Trump claimed he could rapidly negotiate a new deal with Iran while simultaneously maintaining his stance that maximum pressure was the correct approach.
statements.factCheckDetail: The claim of reaching a deal within one week is unsubstantiated and contradicts the complexity of nuclear negotiations, which historically take months or years. No framework for such rapid negotiations was ever presented.

statements.text: Iran understands only strength. When I was president, they didn't dare act up. Now look at the chaos.
statements.context: During a Super Tuesday victory speech, Trump contrasted his administration's approach to Iran with that of the Biden administration, claiming deterrence had collapsed.
statements.factCheckDetail: While some Iranian proxy activity decreased during specific periods, Iran continued enrichment and proxy operations throughout the Trump presidency, including attacks on Saudi oil facilities in 2019. The framing omits significant escalation events like the Soleimani strike aftermath.

statements.text: If Iran does anything to harm Israel, there will be consequences like nobody has ever seen before.
statements.context: In the final weeks of the 2024 presidential campaign, Trump issued a strong warning to Iran following increased tensions in the region around the anniversary of the October 7 attacks.
statements.factCheckDetail: As a candidate at the time, Trump had no authority to enforce this threat. The statement was interpreted as campaign rhetoric rather than a concrete policy commitment.

statements.text: Maximum pressure is back and it's stronger than ever. Iran's economy will collapse if they don't come to the table.
statements.context: In his first major foreign policy address after returning to office, Trump announced the reimposition of broad sanctions on Iran and signaled willingness to negotiate a new deal.
statements.factCheckDetail: While new sanctions were imposed, Iran had already adapted to sanctions through alternative trade networks with China and Russia. Economic analysts noted the impact would be less severe than the first round of maximum pressure due to these workarounds.

statements.text: I told Bibi, you do what you have to do. America stands with Israel, one hundred percent.
statements.context: After reports surfaced of Israeli intelligence indicating accelerated Iranian enrichment, Trump publicly affirmed U.S. support for Israel's security decisions, which critics said amounted to a green light for preemptive strikes.
statements.factCheckDetail: The statement accurately reflected the administration's public posture of unconditional support for Israeli security decisions, as confirmed by multiple White House officials.

statements.text: We don't want war with Iran. Nobody wants war less than me. But if they keep pushing, all options are on the table.
statements.context: Responding to questions about military buildup in the Persian Gulf, Trump attempted to balance his stated desire for peace with warnings about military readiness, a contradiction critics highlighted.
statements.factCheckDetail: The statement contradicts concurrent actions including carrier group deployments to the Persian Gulf and expanded military aid to Israel. The phrase 'all options on the table' is traditionally understood as an implicit threat of military force.

statements.text: Iran has been warned. They chose this path. What happens next is on them, not on us.
statements.context: Following the initiation of Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities, Trump framed the escalation as entirely Iran's responsibility, despite criticism that U.S. diplomatic disengagement contributed to the crisis.
statements.factCheckDetail: Attribution of sole responsibility to Iran is disputed. Multiple analysts point to the collapse of diplomatic channels, withdrawal from the JCPOA, and explicit U.S. support for Israeli military action as contributing factors to the escalation.

statements.text: We are working to bring this to a close very quickly. It will be a tremendous outcome, believe me.
statements.context: As the conflict entered its third week with expanding scope, Trump claimed to be personally managing de-escalation efforts, though no concrete diplomatic framework was announced.
statements.factCheckDetail: No evidence of active negotiations or a de-escalation framework was presented. The claim of an imminent resolution contradicted reports from diplomatic sources indicating no back-channel communications were underway.

statements.text: Good reason needed for ground troops, but everything is on the table. We will finish the job.
statements.context: Bloomberg interview suggesting ground invasion possible.

statements.text: Iran has two choices: unconditional surrender or we finish the job. There is no third option.
statements.context: Press conference at White House.

statements.text: We've already won in many ways, but we haven't won enough. The new so-called supreme leader is a lightweight. He's going to have to get approval from us — if he doesn't, he's not going to last long.
statements.context: Phone interview with Times of Israel. Called war a 'short-term excursion' and said ending it would be a 'mutual' decision with Netanyahu. Said he has someone in mind to lead Iran.
statements.factCheckDetail: The claim of having 'already won' is premature given Iran continues retaliatory strikes and Hormuz remains closed. The assertion of authority over Iran's leadership selection contradicts international norms of sovereignty.

statements.text: This is a little excursion. It'll be finished pretty quickly. We're doing tremendously well.
statements.context: March 9 press conference. Contradicts Hegseth's signaling of a 4-8 week campaign. Markets remain uncertain about conflict timeline.
statements.factCheckDetail: Characterizing a multi-front war involving 3 carrier groups, 45,000+ deployed troops, 20% of global oil supply disrupted, and 1,300+ confirmed deaths as a 'little excursion' significantly understates the scale and complexity of operations.

statements.text: I think the war is very complete, pretty much. We'll decide together with Israel when it ends. Washington will have the final say.
statements.context: Trump said the war could be over 'pretty quickly' while simultaneously Hegseth warned of the 'most intense day of strikes' yet. Trump said he was 'not happy' with Mojtaba Khamenei and indicated he was open to a Venezuela-style outcome where the regime remains but cooperates.
statements.factCheckDetail: Calling the war 'very complete' contradicts active military operations and Hegseth's announcement of escalated strikes. No ceasefire is in sight and Iran has rejected all mediation attempts.

statements.text: We've destroyed 80 percent of their missile launchers. The war will be over pretty quickly, very soon. But not this week. If they disrupt oil flow any further, we'll increase strikes twentyfold.
statements.context: Trump told Republican Congressional members the war could end 'very soon' while warning Iran against further oil disruption. Called the conflict a 'little excursion.' Oil prices crashed 11-12% on his remarks. Gap widening between US (wanting to wind down) and Israeli (wanting to continue) endgames.
statements.factCheckDetail: 80% of missile launchers claim aligns with CENTCOM's 5,000+ targets struck. However, Iran continues firing projectiles and has rejected all ceasefire overtures. Netanyahu vows 'many surprises' suggesting Israel plans to continue.

statements.text: There's essentially nothing left to target. We've hit 90 percent of their capability. Iran has been set back 50 years.
statements.context: Day 12 comments to reporters. Trump signaled satisfaction with the campaign while Iran deployed Khoramshahr super-heavy missiles — its most powerful conventional weapon — for the first time. 3 more ships attacked at Hormuz. IEA approved record 400M barrel reserve release.
statements.factCheckDetail: While 90% missile reduction aligns with CENTCOM data, Iran demonstrated continued capability by deploying Khoramshahr missiles and attacking 3 more ships. The claim of 'nothing left to target' contradicts ongoing Iranian retaliatory attacks.

statements.text: The Iranian regime is the single greatest state sponsor of terrorism in the world and must be confronted.
statements.context: As a senator on the Foreign Relations Committee, Rubio delivered a floor speech advocating for stronger measures against Iran, aligning with his long-standing hawkish position.
statements.factCheckDetail: While the U.S. State Department does designate Iran as a state sponsor of terrorism, the characterization of 'single greatest' is a subjective ranking. Iran's role is significant but other state actors also contribute to global instability.

statements.text: Any deal with Iran that does not address ballistic missiles and regional aggression is worse than no deal at all.
statements.context: Rubio criticized ongoing European diplomatic efforts to revive nuclear talks with Iran, arguing that a narrow focus on nuclear enrichment ignored larger security threats.
statements.factCheckDetail: This is a policy opinion rather than a factual claim. Nonproliferation experts are divided on whether linking missile programs to nuclear talks helps or hinders diplomatic progress.

statements.text: Iran's proxies have American blood on their hands. Hezbollah, Hamas, the Houthis — all roads lead back to Tehran.
statements.context: Following attacks on U.S. military positions in Syria and Iraq by Iran-backed militias, Rubio called for direct retaliation against Iranian assets.
statements.factCheckDetail: While Iran provides support to these groups, each operates with varying degrees of autonomy. Characterizing all proxy actions as directly ordered by Tehran oversimplifies complex regional dynamics. Responsibility for specific attacks requires individual attribution.

statements.text: As Secretary of State, I will ensure that America's adversaries understand that the era of appeasement is over.
statements.context: In his first major address after confirmation as Secretary of State, Rubio signaled a dramatic shift in diplomatic posture, explicitly naming Iran, China, and Russia.
statements.factCheckDetail: The characterization of previous administrations' policies as 'appeasement' is a political framing. The JCPOA and related diplomatic efforts are viewed by their architects as strategic engagement, not appeasement.

statements.text: We have exhausted every diplomatic avenue with Iran. They have chosen escalation at every turn.
statements.context: Rubio defended the administration's decision to end all back-channel communications with Iran, a move criticized by career diplomats as premature.
statements.factCheckDetail: Multiple diplomatic channels remained available but were not pursued. European allies and Oman had offered to mediate, and Iran's foreign ministry had signaled openness to preliminary discussions. The claim of exhausting every avenue is contradicted by these documented opportunities.

statements.text: Israel has an inherent right to self-defense. We will not second-guess our ally when it faces an existential threat.
statements.context: Responding to IAEA reports of Iran reaching near-weapons-grade enrichment levels, Rubio preemptively endorsed potential Israeli military action.
statements.factCheckDetail: Israel does have a right to self-defense under Article 51 of the UN Charter. However, legal scholars debate whether preemptive strikes against nuclear facilities qualify under this provision without an imminent armed attack.

statements.text: This administration provided intelligence support to Israel as any ally would. We did not plan or direct these operations.
statements.context: After Israeli strikes began, Rubio clarified the U.S. role, attempting to distinguish between intelligence sharing and direct operational involvement amid international criticism.
statements.factCheckDetail: While the U.S. may not have directly planned strike packages, extensive intelligence sharing, refueling support for Israeli aircraft, and satellite data constitute significant operational enablement that goes beyond routine allied cooperation.

statements.text: We are urging all parties to exercise restraint while maintaining our ironclad commitment to Israel's security.
statements.context: Rubio's statement attempted to thread the needle between calls for de-escalation from the international community and continued support for Israeli operations, drawing criticism for being contradictory.
statements.factCheckDetail: Critics pointed out the contradiction between calling for restraint while simultaneously authorizing emergency weapons shipments to Israel. The U.S. also vetoed a ceasefire resolution at the same Security Council session.

statements.text: Our military is the most powerful force in history. Any adversary who tests us will regret it.
statements.context: In his first Pentagon press briefing, Hegseth emphasized military readiness and deterrence, setting a combative tone for his tenure.
statements.factCheckDetail: While the U.S. military is widely considered the world's most capable, the characterization of 'most powerful in history' is a subjective claim. Readiness assessments from the GAO have identified specific capability gaps.

statements.text: Iran's air defense systems are outdated Soviet-era relics. They would not survive a serious engagement.
statements.context: In an interview, Hegseth appeared to dismiss Iranian military capabilities, a statement that drew pushback from military analysts who noted Iran's investment in indigenous defense systems.
statements.factCheckDetail: Iran has significantly modernized its air defense network, including the domestically produced Bavar-373 system and Russian-supplied S-300 batteries. Military analysts assess these systems as capable of posing meaningful threats to attacking aircraft.

statements.text: We have positioned our assets to ensure freedom of navigation in the Strait of Hormuz. Commerce will not be disrupted.
statements.context: Hegseth announced the deployment of an additional carrier strike group to the Persian Gulf amid rising tensions and Iranian naval exercises near the Strait.
statements.factCheckDetail: While the U.S. deployed additional naval assets, Iran's extensive arsenal of anti-ship missiles, fast attack boats, and mine-laying capabilities mean a guarantee of uninterrupted commerce is not militarily supportable. Shipping insurance rates rose 400% in this period.

statements.text: Our troops in the region are positioned defensively. We are not seeking conflict but we are prepared for any scenario.
statements.context: Hegseth sought to reassure Congress after reports indicated U.S. force levels in the Middle East had reached their highest point since 2003.
statements.factCheckDetail: The characterization of deployments as purely 'defensive' was questioned when leaked operational plans showed pre-positioned strike assets and forward-deployed special operations units. The buildup exceeded what military experts considered necessary for a purely defensive posture.

statements.text: We provided no direct military support for Israeli operations. U.S. forces are not engaged in combat operations against Iran.
statements.context: Hours before Israeli strikes commenced, Hegseth denied U.S. combat involvement, though reports later indicated U.S. aerial refueling tankers supported Israeli strike aircraft.
statements.factCheckDetail: While U.S. forces may not have directly fired weapons, aerial refueling of Israeli strike aircraft, intelligence sharing, and AWACS support constitute meaningful military support for combat operations, even if not classified as direct combat engagement.

statements.text: Iranian ballistic missile capabilities have been significantly degraded. The threat to our regional partners is diminished.
statements.context: Hegseth provided a battle damage assessment claiming Israeli and allied operations had destroyed a substantial portion of Iran's missile infrastructure.
statements.factCheckDetail: Independent verification of battle damage claims has not been possible due to access restrictions. Iran maintains dispersed and hardened missile facilities, and intelligence agencies have historically struggled to assess underground facility damage from satellite imagery alone.

statements.text: Force protection of our 45,000 personnel in the region remains my top priority. We are ready for any contingency.
statements.context: As Iranian-aligned militia attacks on U.S. bases in Iraq and Syria intensified, Hegseth confirmed troop numbers that had not been officially disclosed, drawing criticism for the operational security lapse.
statements.factCheckDetail: The troop figure, while not previously confirmed, was broadly consistent with independent estimates. The statement of prioritizing force protection is consistent with official DoD policy.

statements.text: Operation Epic Fury is on track as we enter the second week. We have spared no expense or capability on air defense, but we can't stop everything.
statements.context: Pentagon briefing on Day 10. Signaled the conflict could last 4-8 weeks. Dismissed criticisms of the war effort while acknowledging limits of air defense systems.
statements.factCheckDetail: The acknowledgment of air defense limitations is consistent with reports of successful Iranian strikes on Gulf state infrastructure. The 4-8 week timeline represents first official duration estimate.

statements.text: Iran is the greatest threat to world peace. A nuclear Iran is an existential threat to the State of Israel and to all civilization.
statements.context: In an address to a joint session of Congress, Netanyahu framed the Iran nuclear issue as a global civilizational threat, seeking to build international support for action.
statements.factCheckDetail: While Iran's nuclear program poses legitimate proliferation concerns, characterizing it as the 'greatest threat to world peace' and 'all civilization' is considered hyperbolic by most international security analysts, who cite multiple concurrent global threats.

statements.text: Israel will not allow Iran to acquire nuclear weapons. We reserve the right to act alone if necessary.
statements.context: At the UN General Assembly, Netanyahu reiterated Israel's red line on Iranian nuclear capability, signaling willingness for unilateral military action.
statements.factCheckDetail: This accurately represents Israel's longstanding stated policy position. Israel's Begin Doctrine, established after the 1981 Osirak reactor strike, holds that Israel will prevent hostile states from acquiring nuclear weapons.

statements.text: Iran's attack on Israeli soil was an unprecedented act of aggression. We will respond at a time and manner of our choosing.
statements.context: Following Iran's direct missile and drone attack on Israel in April 2024, Netanyahu addressed the nation and pledged a calculated response.
statements.factCheckDetail: Iran did conduct a direct military strike on Israeli territory in April 2024, the first such direct attack. The characterization of the event as unprecedented and aggressive is factually supported.

statements.text: We welcome the return of President Trump. Israel has never had a greater friend in the White House.
statements.context: After the 2024 U.S. election, Netanyahu praised Trump's victory and anticipated closer coordination on Iran policy.
statements.factCheckDetail: The characterization of Trump as Israel's greatest White House friend is subjective. While Trump recognized Jerusalem and the Golan Heights, other presidents have provided significant support including military aid packages and diplomatic backing.

statements.text: Our intelligence indicates Iran is months away from a nuclear weapon. The world cannot afford to wait.
statements.context: Netanyahu presented intelligence briefings to allied leaders claiming Iran had crossed critical enrichment thresholds, building the case for preventive action.
statements.factCheckDetail: Israeli intelligence on Iran's nuclear timeline has historically been contested. Previous claims of imminent Iranian nuclear capability dating back to the 1990s did not materialize. Independent IAEA assessments noted increased enrichment but did not confirm weaponization timelines.

statements.text: Two years after October 7th, Israel is stronger and more determined than ever. We will eliminate every threat to our existence.
statements.context: On the second anniversary of the October 7 attacks, Netanyahu connected the ongoing Iran threat to the broader security environment, using the memorial to justify expanded military operations.
statements.factCheckDetail: Claims of increased strength are difficult to independently verify. While Israel's military capabilities remain formidable, the prolonged multi-front conflicts have strained resources and personnel according to IDF reserve force assessments.

statements.text: We will not live under the shadow of a nuclear threat. Tonight, Israel acts to ensure its survival for generations.
statements.context: Netanyahu's address to the nation as Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear facilities commenced, framing the operation as an existential necessity.
statements.factCheckDetail: While the nuclear threat concern has some basis, the characterization of the strikes as purely defensive is contested. Critics argue Israel possesses its own undeclared nuclear arsenal, creating a deterrence dynamic that the 'survival' framing does not acknowledge.

statements.text: Our operations have been precise and limited to military and nuclear targets. We have no quarrel with the Iranian people.
statements.context: Amid reports of civilian casualties near targeted facilities, Netanyahu defended the operation's targeting methodology and attempted to separate the conflict from the Iranian civilian population.
statements.factCheckDetail: While strikes may have targeted military and nuclear facilities, independent reports from humanitarian organizations documented civilian casualties in surrounding areas. Infrastructure damage also affected civilian populations through power and water disruptions.

statements.text: We are open to a ceasefire if Iran permanently dismantles its nuclear program and its proxy network. These are reasonable terms.
statements.context: Under growing international pressure, Netanyahu set conditions for a ceasefire that were widely seen as non-starters designed to appear diplomatic while ensuring continued operations.
statements.factCheckDetail: Demanding permanent dismantlement of both nuclear programs and proxy networks as preconditions for ceasefire is considered diplomatically unrealistic. Analysts note these conditions exceed what was achieved through years of JCPOA negotiations and are designed to be unacceptable.

statements.text: Israel will continue wide-scale strikes on Iran's military and energy infrastructure until the threat is eliminated.
statements.context: IDF briefing.

statements.text: We are breaking their bones. The moment of truth is close. We are trying to free Iran. There will be many surprises in the next phase.
statements.context: Netanyahu addressed the Iranian people directly, vowing 'many surprises.' Diverging from Trump's 'war ending soon' signals — Netanyahu wants to continue operations while Trump pushes de-escalation. Bloomberg reports widening gap between US and Israeli endgames.
statements.factCheckDetail: Netanyahu frames strikes as liberation of Iranians, but 1,380+ civilians killed undermines this framing. IDF has struck 170+ munitions in Tehran, Isfahan, Shiraz including IRGC Quds Force HQ.

statements.text: The Zionist regime is a cancerous tumor that the resistance front will ultimately uproot. This is a divine promise.
statements.context: In an address marking the anniversary of the Islamic Revolution, Khamenei used extreme rhetoric about Israel while framing Iranian regional strategy in religious terms.
statements.factCheckDetail: This is ideological and religious rhetoric rather than a factual claim. It represents the Supreme Leader's longstanding stated position on Israel, though the practical implications of such statements are debated among analysts.

statements.text: America's maximum pressure failed before and it will fail again. Iran's economy has proven resilient against sanctions.
statements.context: Responding to renewed U.S. sanctions rhetoric, Khamenei pointed to Iran's adaptation to previous sanctions as evidence of resilience.
statements.factCheckDetail: While Iran's economy did not collapse under sanctions, it suffered significant contraction, currency devaluation, and reduced oil exports. 'Resilient' masks genuine economic hardship experienced by ordinary Iranians, including inflation exceeding 40%.

statements.text: Our response to Israeli aggression demonstrated that Iran will defend itself. The era of unanswered attacks is over.
statements.context: After Iran's direct missile and drone attack on Israel, Khamenei framed the operation as a new deterrence paradigm, claiming Iran had established a precedent of direct retaliation.
statements.factCheckDetail: Iran's April 2024 attack on Israel did represent a significant escalation and departure from Iran's previous policy of operating primarily through proxies. The characterization of establishing a new norm of direct retaliation is factually supported by the event.

statements.text: Trump's return changes nothing. Iran does not negotiate under threats. Our nuclear program is peaceful and non-negotiable.
statements.context: On the day of Trump's inauguration, Khamenei issued a statement rejecting any preconditions for negotiations and reaffirming Iran's stated position on its nuclear program.
statements.factCheckDetail: The claim that Iran's nuclear program is entirely peaceful is contradicted by IAEA reports documenting enrichment to 60% purity, far beyond civilian energy needs. Iran has also restricted IAEA inspector access, undermining transparency claims.

statements.text: We possess the technical capability to defend our nation by any means necessary. Our scientists' achievements cannot be bombed away.
statements.context: In a statement widely interpreted as a veiled reference to nuclear weapons capability, Khamenei used ambiguous language that stopped short of declaring a weapons program while implying deterrent capacity.
statements.factCheckDetail: The deliberately ambiguous language makes fact-checking difficult. Intelligence agencies disagree on whether Iran has achieved weapons-breakout capability. The claim that knowledge cannot be destroyed by bombing is generally true regarding scientific expertise, though infrastructure can be damaged.

statements.text: The Zionist enemy has made a grave miscalculation. They have opened the gates of hell upon themselves.
statements.context: Hours after Israeli strikes began on Iranian nuclear facilities, Khamenei issued a brief statement promising severe retaliation.
statements.factCheckDetail: This is a threat and rhetorical statement rather than a verifiable factual claim. The characterization of the strikes as a 'miscalculation' represents Iran's strategic assessment but cannot be independently verified.

statements.text: The resistance axis stands united. Every attack on Iran strengthens our allies' resolve from Beirut to Baghdad to Sana'a.
statements.context: As Iranian-aligned groups across the region launched retaliatory attacks, Khamenei framed the conflict as a validation of Iran's alliance network.
statements.venue: Address to Supreme National Security Council
statements.factCheckDetail: While proxy groups did escalate attacks, reports indicated coordination challenges and some groups acting independently. Hezbollah's capacity had been significantly degraded by prior Israeli operations in Lebanon, undermining the claim of unified strength.

statements.text: We will never surrender. Iran has endured for thousands of years and will outlast this aggression as it has outlasted all others.
statements.context: In a national address as the conflict intensified, Khamenei invoked Persian historical resilience to rally domestic support amid reports of significant infrastructure damage.
statements.factCheckDetail: The historical framing is largely rhetorical. Iran does have a long civilizational history, but the analogy to current geopolitical conflict is more motivational than analytical. The practical outcome of the current conflict remains undetermined.

statements.text: I believe in constructive engagement with the world. Iran's security is best served through diplomacy, not isolation.
statements.context: During his presidential campaign, Pezeshkian distinguished himself from hardline candidates by advocating for renewed diplomatic engagement with the West.
statements.factCheckDetail: This accurately represents Pezeshkian's stated campaign platform, which emphasized diplomatic engagement. His election was widely seen as a public mandate for moderation, though the Supreme Leader retains final authority on foreign policy.

statements.text: Iran is ready to return to the negotiating table without preconditions. We seek a deal that respects our sovereignty and rights.
statements.context: In his debut address at the UN General Assembly as president, Pezeshkian extended an olive branch to Western nations, signaling openness to new nuclear negotiations.
statements.factCheckDetail: While Pezeshkian expressed willingness to negotiate, the Supreme Leader's office subsequently clarified that certain conditions remained, including sanctions relief before substantive talks. The president's authority on nuclear policy is limited compared to the Supreme Leader.

statements.text: The Trump administration has not responded to our diplomatic signals. We cannot negotiate with people who only speak in threats.
statements.context: After months of unanswered diplomatic overtures, Pezeshkian expressed frustration with the lack of U.S. engagement, marking a shift in his initially optimistic tone.
statements.factCheckDetail: Multiple diplomatic sources confirmed that Iranian back-channel communications were not reciprocated by the Trump administration. European mediators corroborated the lack of U.S. engagement with Iranian diplomatic proposals.

statements.text: Our nuclear program serves our energy needs and our scientific advancement. We have no interest in weapons of destruction.
statements.context: Responding to Israeli intelligence claims about Iran's nuclear progress, Pezeshkian maintained the official position that the program was entirely civilian.
statements.factCheckDetail: While Iran claims its nuclear program is peaceful, enrichment to 60% purity significantly exceeds civilian energy requirements (3-5%). IAEA reports have documented activities inconsistent with a purely civilian program, including restricted inspector access to certain facilities.

statements.text: I urge the international community to intervene before it is too late. There are those who want war, and we are not among them.
statements.context: As tensions escalated to critical levels, Pezeshkian made a direct appeal to the UN and European leaders, positioning Iran as seeking peace while Israel and the U.S. pushed toward conflict.
statements.factCheckDetail: While Pezeshkian's stated desire for peace may be genuine, Iran's continued enrichment activities, expansion of proxy operations, and the Supreme Leader's inflammatory rhetoric complicated the credibility of positioning Iran as solely peace-seeking.

statements.text: This unprovoked attack on our sovereign territory is an act of war. We call on all nations to condemn this aggression.
statements.context: Pezeshkian issued a formal statement condemning Israeli strikes, invoking international law and calling for a UN Security Council emergency session.
statements.factCheckDetail: While the strikes did target Iranian sovereign territory, the characterization of 'unprovoked' is contested. Israel cited Iran's nuclear advancement and support for proxy attacks as provocation. The legal classification of the strikes under international law is a matter of ongoing debate.

statements.text: Even now, I call for an immediate ceasefire and international mediation. War serves no one's interests, least of all the people.
statements.context: Breaking from the Supreme Leader's more bellicose rhetoric, Pezeshkian called for ceasefire, highlighting a visible divide between the elected presidency and the Supreme Leader's office.
statements.factCheckDetail: The call for ceasefire is a genuine statement of policy position. The divergence from the Supreme Leader's rhetoric was confirmed by multiple analysts as reflecting a real internal policy disagreement within the Iranian government.

statements.text: Millions of Iranians are displaced and suffering. The humanitarian cost of this conflict demands an immediate end to hostilities.
statements.context: Pezeshkian cited humanitarian concerns as the basis for renewed ceasefire calls, publishing casualty and displacement figures that could not be independently verified.
statements.factCheckDetail: The specific displacement figures cited by Pezeshkian could not be independently verified due to restricted access for international humanitarian organizations. That significant civilian displacement has occurred is widely accepted, but precise numbers remain contested.

statements.text: The erosion of the nuclear nonproliferation framework is deeply alarming. The JCPOA remains the best available path to preventing a nuclear crisis.
statements.context: In his annual report on disarmament, Guterres warned about the consequences of the JCPOA's collapse and urged renewed diplomatic engagement.
statements.factCheckDetail: The assessment that the JCPOA framework was eroding is supported by IAEA reports documenting reduced monitoring access and increased Iranian enrichment. The characterization of the JCPOA as the best available path reflects the consensus view of the nonproliferation community.

statements.text: I condemn the Iranian attack on Israel and call on all parties to step back from the brink. The Middle East cannot afford another war.
statements.context: Guterres responded to Iran's direct attack on Israel with a call for de-escalation, attempting to maintain the UN's position of neutrality while condemning the specific act of aggression.
statements.venue: UN Headquarters Press Statement, New York
statements.factCheckDetail: The condemnation and the characterization of the regional security situation are consistent with documented events. The call for restraint represents the official UN position.

statements.text: The failure of diplomacy is not inevitable. It is a choice made by leaders who prefer confrontation to compromise.
statements.context: Guterres expressed growing frustration with the lack of diplomatic engagement between the U.S., Israel, and Iran, placing responsibility on political leaders.
statements.venue: Address to UN General Assembly Special Session
statements.factCheckDetail: This is a normative statement reflecting the Secretary-General's assessment of diplomatic dynamics. While the characterization of diplomatic failure as a political choice is shared by many analysts, others argue structural factors beyond individual leaders' choices contributed to the breakdown.

statements.text: I am deeply concerned by reports of near-weapons-grade enrichment. I urge Iran to cooperate fully with IAEA inspectors.
statements.context: Responding to an IAEA report indicating enrichment levels approaching weapons-grade, Guterres issued a pointed statement calling for transparency.
statements.factCheckDetail: The IAEA did report enrichment levels nearing weapons-grade. Guterres's call for cooperation with inspectors reflected the official UN position and was supported by the IAEA Board of Governors' own resolutions.

statements.text: Military strikes on nuclear facilities risk catastrophic environmental and humanitarian consequences. I call for an immediate cessation of hostilities.
statements.context: Within hours of the first Israeli strikes, Guterres issued an urgent statement warning about the potential for radiological contamination and calling for a ceasefire.
statements.venue: Emergency UN Press Conference, New York
statements.factCheckDetail: The warning about environmental and humanitarian consequences of striking nuclear facilities is supported by scientific assessments from the IAEA and independent nuclear safety experts. Radiological contamination from damaged enrichment facilities is a documented risk.

statements.text: The Security Council's inability to act is a failure of the international system. Civilians are dying while permanent members exercise vetoes.
statements.context: After the U.S. vetoed a ceasefire resolution, Guterres took the unusual step of publicly criticizing the Security Council, reflecting deep frustration with institutional paralysis.
statements.venue: UN General Assembly Emergency Special Session
statements.factCheckDetail: The U.S. did veto a ceasefire resolution. Guterres's characterization of institutional failure reflects factual events. The criticism of the veto mechanism, while politically charged, is grounded in the documented inability of the Council to pass a binding resolution.

statements.text: International humanitarian law applies to all parties in this conflict. Targeting civilian infrastructure is a violation regardless of the justification offered.
statements.context: Guterres responded to reports of strikes hitting power plants, water treatment facilities, and transportation infrastructure in both Iran and Israel.
statements.factCheckDetail: The statement accurately reflects international humanitarian law, specifically the Fourth Geneva Convention and Additional Protocol I, which prohibit attacks on civilian infrastructure. The legal principles cited are well-established in international law.

statements.text: I am appointing a Special Envoy and proposing a 72-hour humanitarian pause. The suffering must stop. We need space for diplomacy.
statements.context: Guterres announced concrete UN-led de-escalation measures, including the appointment of a Special Envoy and a proposed humanitarian corridor agreement.
statements.factCheckDetail: The appointment of a Special Envoy and the proposal for a humanitarian pause are concrete actions within the Secretary-General's authority. Whether parties will accept the proposal remains to be seen.

speakers.name: Kaja Kallas
speakers.title: EU High Representative for Foreign Affairs

statements.text: Europe must develop an independent strategic posture on Iran. We cannot simply follow Washington's lead on every crisis.
statements.context: As incoming EU High Representative, Kallas signaled a more autonomous European approach to the Iran file, distinguishing EU policy from the incoming Trump administration.
statements.factCheckDetail: The statement accurately reflects the EU's stated intent to maintain strategic autonomy on foreign policy. The EU had already diverged from U.S. Iran policy during the first Trump administration by creating the INSTEX mechanism to preserve trade.

statements.text: The JCPOA was imperfect but it was working. Europe remains committed to a diplomatic solution based on its framework.
statements.context: Kallas reaffirmed European commitment to the diplomatic approach embodied in the JCPOA, contrasting the EU position with the U.S. withdrawal and maximum pressure strategy.
statements.factCheckDetail: The claim that the JCPOA 'was working' is partially supported by IAEA data showing Iran was compliant before the U.S. withdrawal. However, the deal had acknowledged limitations including sunset clauses, and Iran had begun exceeding limits even before full U.S. withdrawal took effect.

statements.text: We have opened a direct diplomatic channel with Tehran. Europe will not stand by while others march toward conflict.
statements.context: Kallas announced EU-Iran diplomatic talks, positioning Europe as an active mediator while the U.S. refused direct engagement with Iran.
statements.factCheckDetail: The EU did establish direct diplomatic contacts with Iran through the EEAS. This was confirmed by both European and Iranian diplomatic sources and represented a significant divergence from U.S. policy.

statements.text: Europe will not participate in or support military action against Iran. We believe this path leads only to catastrophe.
statements.context: As military planning accelerated, Kallas explicitly ruled out European participation, creating a significant rift with the U.S. within the NATO alliance.
statements.factCheckDetail: This accurately represents the official EU position as communicated to NATO allies. The statement created documented tensions within the alliance, with the U.S. expressing disappointment at the lack of allied solidarity.

statements.text: Our mediation efforts have produced a framework for talks. All we need is political will from both sides to take the final step.
statements.context: Kallas revealed that European shuttle diplomacy had produced a draft framework for negotiations, but neither Israel nor Iran had committed to participating.
statements.factCheckDetail: The existence of a draft framework was confirmed by European diplomatic sources but its contents were not made public. Claims of being close to a breakthrough were disputed by both Israeli and U.S. officials who described the framework as inadequate.

statements.text: We condemn these strikes in the strongest possible terms. Unilateral military action undermines international law and regional stability.
statements.context: The EU issued one of the sharpest criticisms of Israeli military action, straining EU-Israel relations and drawing criticism from the U.S. for failing to support an ally.
statements.venue: EU Emergency Foreign Affairs Council Statement
statements.factCheckDetail: The condemnation reflects the official EU position. The legal assessment that unilateral strikes may violate international law is supported by many international law scholars, though Israel disputes this characterization based on self-defense provisions.

statements.text: Europe is imposing targeted sanctions on entities involved in the escalation. We are also tripling our humanitarian aid to affected civilian populations.
statements.context: The EU announced a dual-track response combining punitive measures with humanitarian support, positioning Europe as a distinct voice from both the U.S. and China/Russia.
statements.factCheckDetail: The EU did announce a sanctions package and increased humanitarian aid. The specific aid figure and sanctions targets were detailed in official EU Council documents.

statements.text: We are working with the UN Special Envoy on a European peace initiative. A ceasefire must be the first step, not the last.
statements.context: Kallas announced EU coordination with the newly appointed UN Special Envoy, presenting a joint European-UN diplomatic framework as an alternative to the stalled U.S.-led process.
statements.venue: Joint Press Conference with UN Special Envoy, Geneva
statements.factCheckDetail: EU-UN coordination on a peace initiative was confirmed by both organizations. The existence of a diplomatic framework document was verified, though its prospects for acceptance by the warring parties remained uncertain.

statements.text: The Islamic Republic will not rest until the aggressors are expelled from our region. My father's blood will not have been spilled in vain.
statements.context: First address as Supreme Leader. 56-year-old mid-ranking religious scholar with close IRGC ties, seen as even more hardline than his father.

statements.text: Iran is prepared for a long war. We will respond to every aggression tenfold. The era of American dominance in our region is over.
statements.context: Statement to CNN via Iranian officials. Putin pledged 'unwavering' support; China opposed targeting the new leader.

statements.text: There will be no ceasefire. There are no contacts with the United States. Iran will defend itself by any means necessary.
statements.context: Press conference in Tehran.

statements.text: Iran has no interest in negotiations while under attack. Mediation requests from other countries have been rejected.
statements.context: Foreign Ministry spokesperson statement. Despite Oman pushing for mediation and President Pezeshkian acknowledging 'some countries have begun mediation efforts.'

statements.text: We need to continue fighting for the sake of our people. Iran will not accept any ceasefire while our territory is under attack.
statements.context: NBC News interview broadcast March 9. Araghchi rejected ceasefire demands and emphasized Iran's commitment to continued resistance.

statements.text: This is Operation Epic Mistake — a misadventure engineered by Israel and paid for by ordinary Americans. We have very bitter experience with US negotiations. Iran will fight as long as it takes.
statements.context: PBS NewsHour interview. Araghchi called the strikes a failure, ruled out negotiations with the US, and vowed continued missile strikes. Also stated: 'They failed at rapid regime change.'
statements.factCheckDetail: Calling it a 'failure' is contradicted by CENTCOM data showing 5,000+ targets struck and 90% reduction in Iranian missile launches. However, regime change has not occurred and Iran continues retaliating.

statements.text: Iran has now deployed its most powerful weapons including the Khoramshahr super-heavy missile. The enemy should understand this is only the beginning of our response capabilities.
statements.context: Day 12 statement following Iran's 37th wave of attacks using Khoramshahr super-heavy ballistic missiles, Iran's most powerful conventional weapon.

statements.text: I urge an immediate end to violence in Iran and across the Middle East. War is always a defeat for humanity.
statements.context: Address from Vatican.

statements.text: I call for the roar of bombs to cease. Humanity must choose dialogue over destruction.
statements.context: Vatican Angelus address. Called for 'a space for dialogue to open.' Tone described as 'relatively restrained' compared to John Paul II on Iraq War.

statements.text: I express deep sorrow for all those killed and wounded. The tragedy unfolding must stop before it becomes an irreparable abyss.
statements.context: New Vatican appeal March 10. Cardinal McElroy (Washington) stated the war does not meet 'minimum criteria' for being morally just. Pope Leo XIV has become a leading voice calling for cessation of hostilities.

statements.text: The attacks on Iran constitute violations of international humanitarian law. Switzerland has a duty to speak.
statements.context: Official government statement.
